Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Off Spin, averaging 84.2 kph (up to 91.8).
  • Typical length is 4-5m (their good length); median 4.5 m.
  • Stock ball is full outside off, turning in, drifting away, hitting the top of off (27% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 15% of the time against right-handers.
  • Turns it 3.2° on average; 12% of balls turn ≥5°.
  • Across an over he holds a fairly consistent length.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Good Length (44 wkts, 1.8% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: Wide of 6th (over the wicket).
  • Takes 16% of wickets pitching a good length on the 6th stump, over the wicket.
  • Beats the bat 3.0% of tracked balls (false-shot 7.4%).
  • Wickets come mostly caught in the field (55%).
  • 24% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to square leg: short leg, keeper, slip: 1st.
  • Very repeatable with his length — using your feet to change his length is more effective than waiting for a loose ball.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching full on the 5th stump (13% of runs).
  • Away from good length, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak through the leg side (37%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes full wide outside off (economy 3.7, beats the bat only 11%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs right-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — full outside off, turning in, drifting away, hitting the top of off (27% of deliveries, economy 2.61). Minor variations: a good length outside off (20%) and full in the channel (11%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %
full outside off ▶turning in, drifting away27%2.61226%
a good length outside off ▶turning in, drifting away20%2.80227%
full in the channel ▶turning in, drifting away11%2.8587%
full on the stumps ▶turning in, drifting away11%3.01116%
full wide outside off ▶turning in, drifting away9%3.65911%
a good length in the channel ▶turning in, drifting away6%3.3947%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs right-handers)

44% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 20 balls; most runs go to the leg side (37%); the drive hurts most (77 fours/sixes at 2.1 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at stumps.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the leg side (66%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it full on the 5th stump most often (13%); rarely drops short (0%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched a good length on the 6th stump, but strikes most often pitching wide outside off.

Speed & Spells

He drops ~1.8 km/h from his opening spell to later spells, keeps his pace into the 2nd innings, and is as quick on the final day as day one.

1st vs 2nd innings of the match.

By match day — fatigue across the game.

Sequencing & Over Construction

Consistent with his length (length spread ±1.1 m; more repeatable than 73% of spin bowlers); holds a steady length right through the over; and holds the same crease position through the over.
Takes his wickets with the stock ball rather than obvious set-ups.
Across 140 dismissals, his wicket ball is close in length and pace to the delivery before it — he builds pressure with repetition, not a big change-up.

Ball 64.8 m0%2.910.9%
Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A low, skiddy release point (lower than 81% of right-arm spin); releases tight to the stumps (54 cm out, tighter than 80% of right-arm spin); varies his spot on the crease a lot (more than 76% of right-arm spin). Comes wider round the wicket (54 cm over vs 91 cm round).
Crease position% ballsBallsEconWktsAvgSR
